refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated response protocols to minimize mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), maintainability (DP2), and fault management (DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time budgets.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (a) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (b) modular design enabling rapid component replacement, and (c) comprehensive fault detection and automated recovery protocols, with explicit allocation of downtime budgets to maintenance windows and fault recovery phases to achieve ≥0.97 availability target.

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP1) and modular design (DP2) are orthogonal; redundancy addresses fault masking while modularity addresses repair speed—both required for high availability.
FDIA (DP6) and failover logic (DP1) must coordinate: failover triggers must be synchronized with fault detection to avoid cascading failures or false positives.
Predictive maintenance (DP3) and graceful degradation (DP4) are complementary but not tightly coupled; PM reduces fault frequency while degradation preserves availability when faults occur.
Distributed control (DP5) introduces complexity in synchronization and state consistency; if selected, must be carefully integrated with redundancy strategy to avoid split-brain scenarios.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 integrates three semi-independent concerns (fault masking, repair speed, fault response) with clear interfaces. Coupling is manageable if redundancy boundaries, failover triggers, and recovery protocols are explicitly defined. Risk: over-specification of all three simultaneously may exceed cost/complexity constraints; phased implementation recommended.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target of ≥0.97 availability is achievable with NGHE operational constraints (e.g., worksite access, environmental conditions, supply chain for spare parts).
RISK: Redundancy cost and weight/volume penalties may conflict with NGHE deployment constraints; trade-off analysis required.
RISK: Automatic failover logic introduces new failure modes (false positives, failover delays); FDIA system must be independently validated.
RISK: Modular design and hot-swappability require standardized interfaces and connector reliability; interface failures could negate modularity benefits.
RISK: Predictive maintenance depends on accurate condition monitoring; sensor drift or model inaccuracy could lead to missed faults or unnecessary maintenance.

RISK: Availability target is measured over 'agreed evaluation period'—duration and operational profile (continuous vs. intermittent use) must be clearly specified to avoid ambiguity in acceptance testing.
RISK: Worksite safety plan may impose operational constraints (e.g., shutdown windows, personnel availability) that reduce effective uptime; must be factored into availability budget.
RISK: Supply chain resilience for spare parts and replacement modules is not addressed; long lead times could violate MTTR targets.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) evaluation period duration and operational profile (e.g., 8 hours/day, 5 days/week vs. continuous), (2) definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., excludes planned maintenance, includes graceful degradation), (3) acceptable downtime budget per fault type (e.g., <15 min for failover, <4 hours for component replacement), and (4) constraints on redundancy (e.g., weight, cost, power budget). DP7 should be decomposed into three sub-DPs with explicit interface specifications: (a) Redundancy architecture with failover decision logic, (b) Modularity specification with connector/interface standards, (c) FDIA and recovery protocol with defined MTTR targets. Recommend phased validation: first demonstrate FDIA and MTTR targets on single-fault scenarios, then validate redundancy failover under controlled conditions, then integrate and test under representative worksite operational profile.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability architecture encompassing redundancy management, fault detection, automated recovery, and downtime budgeting across all subsystems and components within the NGHE platform

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and status signals from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to feed fault detection and availability monitoring

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound failover directives and recovery commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems during fault events

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound critical availability and fault notifications to Safety Services for coordinated response

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for redundancy coordination, state synchronization, and failover signaling across distributed subsystems

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: DP7 is fundamentally a system-level availability strategy that spans all subsystems. NGHE Solution as the integrating platform naturally owns redundancy orchestration, fault detection logic, recovery protocols, and downtime budgeting. Minor diffusion occurs only at subsystem-specific failover implementations (Vehicle motor redundancy, Teleoperation link redundancy) which remain localized.

Poor containment: DP7 is not vehicle-specific. While Vehicle may implement redundant actuators or sensors, the integrated availability architecture, fault detection across all subsystems, automated recovery protocols, and downtime budgeting are system-level concerns that would diffuse across Teleoperation, Safety Services, and network infrastructure.

Poor containment: DP7 spans beyond teleoperation control. Redundancy, fault detection, and recovery must coordinate Vehicle, Safety Services, and network layers. Placing this DP in Teleoperation would fragment availability logic and create tight coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a consumer of availability guarantees, not the owner of the integrated availability architecture. DP7 requires coordination across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and network layers that Safety Services does not control. Placing DP7 here inverts responsibility and creates diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 is a system-level availability strategy that must coordinate redundancy, fault detection, and recovery across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and network infrastructure. Only NGHE Solution, as the integrating platform, has the architectural scope and authority to own this DP without major diffusion. Subsystems implement redundancy at their local level (e.g., dual motors in Vehicle), but the orchestration, monitoring, and recovery logic resides in NGHE Solution.
containment_risks: Risk: Subsystem-specific redundancy implementations (e.g., Vehicle motor failover, Teleoperation link redundancy) may develop independently, creating inconsistent fault detection and recovery semantics. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must define and enforce a unified fault model and recovery protocol that all subsystems conform to.

Risk: Downtime budgeting and maintenance window allocation require coordination with operational scheduling and Safety Services constraints. If NGHE Solution does not have visibility into Safety Services operational policies, availability targets may be unachievable. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must carry downtime constraints and maintenance windows bidirectionally.

Risk: Network Link redundancy and failover may be managed by infrastructure outside NGHE Solution scope. Mitigation: Network Link interface must expose redundancy state and failover readiness to NGHE Solution for integrated availability decisions.

Risk: Automated recovery protocols may conflict with Safety Services safety constraints (e.g., automatic restart of Vehicle during critical operations). Mitigation: Recovery logic must be gated by SafetyAlert feedback and Safety Services authorization.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses the FR by specifying a coherent solution strategy: redundancy + modular design + fault detection + recovery + downtime budgeting. The DP is measurable against the ≥0.97 availability target and operationally concrete. The three pillars (redundancy, modularity, fault detection/recovery) are necessary and sufficient to achieve the stated availability threshold. No logical gaps detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Subsystem-specific redundancy implementations (Vehicle motor failover, Teleoperation link redundancy) may develop independent fault models and recovery semantics. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must define and enforce a unified fault taxonomy and recovery state machine that all subsystems conform to via interface contracts.

Downtime budgeting and maintenance window allocation require bidirectional coordination with Safety Services operational policies and scheduling constraints. If NGHE Solution lacks visibility into Safety Services maintenance windows and safety-critical operation periods, availability targets become unachievable. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must carry downtime constraints and maintenance scheduling metadata bidirectionally.

Network Link redundancy and failover orchestration may be managed by infrastructure (e.g., network control plane, load balancers) outside NGHE Solution direct control. Mitigation: Network Link interface must expose redundancy state, failover readiness, and latency metrics to NGHE Solution for integrated availability decisions.

Automated recovery protocols may conflict with Safety Services safety constraints (e.g., automatic Vehicle restart during critical teleoperation tasks). Mitigation: Recovery logic must be gated by SafetyAlert feedback and require explicit Safety Services authorization before executing recovery actions.

Minor diffusion at subsystem level (8% diffusion score for NGHE Solution) is acceptable and expected: Vehicle and Teleoperation implement local redundancy (e.g., dual sensors, link failover) but do not own the integrated orchestration. This is a healthy separation of concerns.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: DP7 is correctly assigned to NGHE Solution (92% containment, 8% diffusion). NGHE Solution as the integrating platform naturally owns redundancy orchestration, unified fault detection logic, automated recovery protocol coordination, and downtime budgeting across all subsystems. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement redundancy at their local level but do not own the system-level availability architecture. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and necessary for NGHE Solution to execute DP7. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or fragmented ownership.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability is directly measurable via telemetry inbound from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. NGHE Solution can aggregate uptime/downtime events, calculate measured availability over the agreed evaluation period, and compare against the ≥0.97 target. Acceptance criteria is quantitative and verifiable. Downtime budgeting allocation to maintenance windows and fault recovery phases can be audited via NGHE Solution logs and SafetyAlert coordination records.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: DP7 allocation to NGHE Solution is clear and unambiguous. Subsystem-level redundancy responsibilities are implicitly allocated to Vehicle (motor/sensor redundancy), Teleoperation (link redundancy), and Safety Services (safety-gated recovery authorization), but the integrated availability architecture and orchestration remain with NGHE Solution. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) establish clear responsibility boundaries. No allocation ambiguity detected.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define a unified fault model and recovery state machine in DP7 documentation. Specify which fault types trigger automatic recovery, which require Safety Services authorization, and which mandate maintenance windows. This prevents subsystem-level diffusion of recovery semantics.

Enhance SafetyAlert interface specification to include bidirectional downtime constraints and maintenance scheduling metadata. NGHE Solution must receive Safety Services operational policies (e.g., no recovery during critical teleoperation tasks) and communicate planned maintenance windows to Safety Services for coordination.

Add Network Link interface requirement: expose redundancy state (active/standby), failover readiness, and latency metrics to NGHE Solution. This ensures NGHE Solution can make integrated availability decisions without blind spots in network infrastructure.

Specify recovery gating logic: automated recovery protocols must be conditional on SafetyAlert feedback. Define the authorization flow and timeout behavior if Safety Services does not respond to recovery requests within a defined window.

Document downtime budget allocation methodology in DP7. Specify how maintenance windows and fault recovery phases are scheduled, how they are tracked against the ≥0.97 target, and how conflicts between planned maintenance and operational demands are resolved.

decision.justification: FR7 (Achieve ≥0.97 system availability) passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) with high confidence. DP7 is logically sound, architecturally well-assigned to NGHE Solution, and measurable. However, the gate results expose five critical diffusion risks and four recommended revisions that are NOT cosmetic—they are structural gaps in the current DP7 specification that must be resolved before decomposition can proceed safely. The recommended revisions explicitly call for: (1) unified fault model and recovery state machine definition, (2) bidirectional SafetyAlert interface enhancement with downtime constraints, (3) Network Link interface exposure of redundancy state and failover metrics, (4) recovery gating logic conditional on SafetyAlert, and (5) downtime budget allocation methodology. These are not implementation details; they are architectural decisions that belong at the current level and must be crystallized before sub-FRs can be meaningfully derived. Proceeding to sub-FRs without resolving these would create orphaned sub-requirements that cannot be traced to a coherent parent DP. Given high safety criticality, the risk of diffusion in fault recovery semantics and Safety Services coordination is unacceptable. REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL is the safer choice, but the gate has already passed; therefore, ZIGZAG_DOWN with explicit sub-FR derivation that forces resolution of the four recommended revisions at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Define and enforce a unified fault taxonomy and recovery state machine that all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) conform to via interface contracts. Specify which fault types trigger automatic recovery, which require Safety Services authorization, and which mandate maintenance windows.

FR7.2: Establish bidirectional SafetyAlert interface specification that carries downtime constraints and maintenance scheduling metadata. NGHE Solution must receive Safety Services operational policies (e.g., no recovery during critical teleoperation tasks) and communicate planned maintenance windows to Safety Services for coordination.

FR7.3: Expose Network Link redundancy state (active/standby), failover readiness, and latency metrics to NGHE Solution via Network Link interface. Enable NGHE Solution to make integrated availability decisions without blind spots in network infrastructure.

FR7.4: Implement recovery gating logic such that automated recovery protocols are conditional on SafetyAlert feedback. Define authorization flow and timeout behavior if Safety Services does not respond to recovery requests within a defined window.

FR7.5: Document and operationalize downtime budget allocation methodology. Specify how maintenance windows and fault recovery phases are scheduled, how they are tracked against the ≥0.97 target, and how conflicts between planned maintenance and operational demands are resolved.

revise.why: DP7 is logically sound but under-specified in four critical areas identified by the gate: (1) fault model and recovery state machine are implicit, not explicit; (2) SafetyAlert interface lacks bidirectional downtime constraint metadata; (3) Network Link interface does not expose redundancy state and failover metrics; (4) recovery gating logic is not formally defined; (5) downtime budget allocation methodology is not documented. These gaps create diffusion risk in subsystem-level implementations and Safety Services coordination. Revise DP7 to explicitly incorporate these four elements as mandatory sub-components of the redundancy + modular design + fault detection/recovery + downtime budgeting strategy. The revision should be captured as DP7 sub-specifications (DP7.1–DP7.5) that map 1:1 to the derived sub-FRs above.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has sufficient visibility and control authority over Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to enforce unified fault semantics and recovery protocols via interface contracts.
SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and capable of carrying structured metadata (downtime constraints, maintenance windows, safety-gated recovery authorization).
Network Link infrastructure (load balancers, network control plane) can expose redundancy state and failover metrics to NGHE Solution in real-time.
Safety Services operational policies (e.g., no recovery during critical teleoperation tasks) are formalized and can be communicated to NGHE Solution via SafetyAlert.
Downtime budget allocation (maintenance windows + fault recovery phases) can be scheduled and tracked without systematic conflicts between planned maintenance and operational demands.

The ≥0.97 availability target is achievable with the proposed redundancy + modular design + fault detection/recovery + downtime budgeting strategy, assuming subsystems implement local redundancy as expected.
open_risks: Subsystem-specific redundancy implementations (Vehicle motor failover, Teleoperation link redundancy) may develop independent fault models and recovery semantics if unified fault taxonomy is not enforced. Mitigation: FR7.1 must define and enforce unified fault taxonomy via interface contracts.

Downtime budgeting and maintenance window allocation may conflict with Safety Services operational policies if bidirectional coordination is not established. Mitigation: FR7.2 must establish bidirectional SafetyAlert interface with downtime constraint metadata.

Network Link redundancy and failover orchestration may be managed by infrastructure outside NGHE Solution direct control, creating blind spots in availability decisions. Mitigation: FR7.3 must expose Network Link redundancy state and failover metrics to NGHE Solution.

Automated recovery protocols may conflict with Safety Services safety constraints (e.g., automatic Vehicle restart during critical teleoperation tasks). Mitigation: FR7.4 must implement recovery gating logic conditional on SafetyAlert feedback.

Downtime budget allocation methodology may not be operationalized, leading to untracked maintenance windows and unachievable availability targets. Mitigation: FR7.5 must document and operationalize downtime budget allocation methodology.

High safety criticality amplifies the risk of diffusion in fault recovery semantics. If subsystems implement recovery protocols independently, safety-critical failures may not be handled consistently. Mitigation: FR7.1 and FR7.4 must enforce unified recovery state machine with explicit Safety Services authorization gating.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of unified fault taxonomy (fault types, severity levels, recovery triggers, authorization requirements) that all subsystems must conform to.
SafetyAlert interface specification document with bidirectional metadata schema (downtime constraints, maintenance scheduling, safety-gated recovery authorization).
Network Link interface specification document with redundancy state exposure (active/standby, failover readiness, latency metrics).
Recovery gating logic specification: authorization flow, timeout behavior, and conditional execution rules for automated recovery protocols.
Downtime budget allocation methodology document: scheduling algorithm, tracking mechanism, conflict resolution policy, and audit trail requirements.
Subsystem-level redundancy implementation plans (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) that confirm alignment with unified fault taxonomy and recovery state machine.
Safety Services operational policy documentation (e.g., no recovery during critical teleoperation tasks) that can be encoded in SafetyAlert interface.
Network infrastructure documentation confirming capability to expose redundancy state and failover metrics to NGHE Solution.
Availability measurement and verification plan: telemetry collection, uptime/downtime calculation, target comparison methodology, and acceptance criteria.
